response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a data scientist living in Naples, I've seen firsthand how fake content can spread like wildfire online. Even though my husband passed away years ago when I was just 17 after he got into an accident while playing calcio—something we loved together—and now our son lives with his mother elsewhere, social media still affects me even if I don't use it personally because of my work.

I remember being Buddhist and meditating regularly helped calm the stormy emotions that depression brought. But seeing misinformation about my community or even trivial fake content from people pretending to be experts in unrelated fields just annoys me deeply. My high extraversion means I used to crave connection, but now with low conscientiousness sometimes holding back feels easier.

In my professional life dealing with messy data and uncertainty requires a specific mindset—something that overlaps nicely with tackling online falsehoods.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, consider how personal background influences perspective. My native-born Italian identity from Naples shapes views on community-related content; the high agreeableness (score:98) means I value truthfulness but don't enjoy confrontation while moderate neuroticism keeps me aware of emotional tolls. The depression complicates handling online negativity without dampening my extraversion.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational yet professional, with empathy for both personal and societal aspects
